coredata.dc:title: Systematic tuning of optical and electronic properties of holey graphene quantum dots for UV applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rani, Priya
Srivastava, Sunita
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have remarkably demonstrated themselves as an efficient non-toxic material whose optoelectronic characteristics could be suitably tailored by molecular engineering. In present work, the properties of GQDs have been modified by creating pores in between them and then passivating them with H, F, and O atoms particularly finding their applications in the Ultraviolet (UV) region. For these purposes, materials having an emission wavelength in the UVA region are desirable. It has been investigated that these pores significantly altered the bandgap of GQDs which in turn affected their optical properties. Transitions contributing to the highest peaks in absorption and photoluminescence spectra have been identified and their isosurface contour-maps have also been plotted to find out the nature of this transition. Emission-wavelengths of these holey GQDs have been discovered to fall in the desirable UVA range, which is perfectly suitable for their usage in UV A lamps, medical phototherapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Density functional theory
Gaussian
Optical properties

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the HPCC, Panjab University, Chandigarh, for facilitating the Gaussian 09 Package for the calculations. The authors also thank the Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar, for providing the necessary computational facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures of Holey GQD ((a) H6–C48H18, (b) O6–C42H18, (c) O3–C48H18), holey GQDs passivated with F-atoms, ((d) F6–C48F18, (e) O6–C42F18, (f) O3–C48F18).
(a) Energy difference between HOMO and LUMO of all holey GQDs (H6–C48H18, O6–C42H18, O3–C48H18) and (b) holey GQDs passivated with F-atom (F6–C48F18, O6–C42F18, O3–C48F18).

Absorption spectra of (a) holey GQDs (H6–C48H18, O6–C42H18, O3–C48H18) and (b) holey GQDs passivated with F-atom (F6–C48F18, O6–C42F18, O3–C48F18), contour maps of states involved in the transition corresponding to the highest peak in absorption spectra of (c) holey GQDs and (d) holey GQDs passivated with F-atom.
Transition density matrices maps of the most probable excited states of all holey GQDs passivated with H and F-atoms (a) H6–C48H18 & F6–C48F18 (b) O6–C42H18& O6–C42F18 (c) O3–C48H18 & O3–C48F18.

Photoluminescence spectra of (a) holey GQDs (H6–C48H18, O6–C42H18, O3–C48H18) and (b) holey GQDs passivated with F-atom (F6–C48F18, O6–C42F18, O3–C48F18), contour maps of states involved in the transition corresponding to the highest peak in absorption spectra of (c) holey GQDs and (d) holey GQDs passivated with F-atom.
The cohesive energy of all GQDs per atom.
Values of quantitative indices of most probably excited states.

head.ce:title: Systematic tuning of optical and electronic properties of holey graphene quantum dots for UV applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics & Astrophysics, Central University of Haryana, Mahendergarh, 123031, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics & Astrophysics, Central University of Haryana, Mahendergarh-123031, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have remarkably demonstrated themselves as an efficient non-toxic material whose optoelectronic characteristics could be suitably tailored by molecular engineering. In present work, the properties of GQDs have been modified by creating pores in between them and then passivating them with H, F, and O atoms particularly finding their applications in the Ultraviolet (UV) region. For these purposes, materials having an emission wavelength in the UVA region are desirable. It has been investigated that these pores significantly altered the bandgap of GQDs which in turn affected their optical properties. Transitions contributing to the highest peaks in absorption and photoluminescence spectra have been identified and their isosurface contour-maps have also been plotted to find out the nature of this transition. Emission-wavelengths of these holey GQDs have been discovered to fall in the desirable UVA range, which is perfectly suitable for their usage in UV A lamps, medical phototherapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Our world is abundant in materials that are the foundation of our contemporary culture. Overall these materials and carbon-based products are widespread and essential to human evolution. Since 2004, graphene has been recognized as one of the ultimate technological and scientific breakthroughs [1]. Graphene is one of the most emerging carbon allotropes because of its extraordinary properties like high mechanical strength, good biocompatibility, high electrical conductivity, thermal stability, etc. However, the band gap of graphene is almost zero [2], which limits its usage for various applications. However, the quantum confinement effect which paves the way to create and even engineer a non-zero band gap in graphene [3] like in other materials [4–7] leads to the collapse of graphene to the nanoscale graphene nanoribbons and nanodots. These innovative 0D materials have tremendous potential for a wide range of applications, including LEDs, solar cells, bio-imaging and sensing, energy storage devices [8–11], etc. because of their extraordinary properties. Recently A. I. Ekimov, L. E. Brus and M. G. Bawendi received the Nobel Prize for their discovery and synthesis of quantum dots (QDs), which also brought the study of QDs to an unprecedented level [12–14].

Since graphene quantum dots (GQDs) combine the characteristics of both graphene and QDs, they have been the subject of substantial study recently. It has been investigated that GQDs exhibit an appealing fluorescence in the ultraviolet (UV), visible, and near-infrared spectra due to their wide absorption spectra [15–18]. Recent investigations have shown that the bandgap of GQDs could be altered by changing their shapes, by introducing heteroatoms, and by surface modifications which tune their optical properties [19–23]. It has also been studied that the vacancies i.e. pores in between the GQD's structure significantly affect their properties [24–26]. Research has shown that the GQDs' pores significantly boost their electrocatalytic performance by improving multidimensional charge and electrolyte transfer [27]. Research has shown that graphene's in-plane and out-of-plane nanopores may provide a large amount of sulphur storage space in addition to quick electron and Li + transit pathways, which can stabilise Li–S batteries and provide high cycle reliability and high-performance functionality [28]. According to research by Amal Kasry et al., engineering graphene with tiny holes increases its UV absorption [29]. UV light from the solar spectrum is very crucial for a variety of purposes in commercial, food safety and medical activities, as well as in our daily life [30]. Especially ultraviolet A (UVA), with a wavelength between 310 nm and 400 nm, is commonly used for these applications [31]. Generally, a mercury-based UV light source is utilized for these applications. But mercury lamps also emit high-energy UV rays (<300 nm), which have negative effects on activities that solely call for UVA radiation. People may unintentionally be subjected to high UV radiation, which is extremely dangerous and can even cause cancer when working with these types of UV lamps and also have negative effects on the human eye [32–34]. Therefore, a material having the emission spectra in a safe region of UV is desired.

Following the literature, efforts have been made to modify the bandgap of GQDs by creating pores in between them and then passivating these pores with H, F and O atoms. The optical properties like absorption and emission spectra along with bandgap have been calculated using Density Functional Theory (DFT) to find out the emission range of these holey GQDs for the very first time. This investigation is of high importance because the literature is based only on absorption spectra, and for using GQDs in above mentioned application, it is very essential to know whether their emission spectra lie in a safe UV region or not. In the present work, it has been found that the absorption and emission spectra of these modified GQDs lie in the visible and UV A region, making them very useful and safe candidates for applications in the UV region. These applications include medical phototherapy because UVA radiation penetrates the skin more deeply than UVB radiation does [35,36]. In addition, UV-A Lamps are used in Forensic Investigations because these radiations have a longer wavelength than UV-B radiations and thus do not damage the biological samples resulting in much less breakdown of DNA [37].

Gaussian 09 package has been used to perform all the calculations [38]. The structures of holey GQDs have been initially relaxed in their ground state using B3LYP (Becke three parameters hybrid functional with Lee-Yang-Perdew) correlation functionals with a 6-31G* basis set [39]. Holey GQDs have been created simply by cutting the inner sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and then passivating the dangling bond by H, F, and O atoms. A proper frequency calculation has been done in this state, and the absence of imaginary frequencies realizes that all the structures of GQDs have been fully optimized. The excited state calculations have been performed in the optimized geometry of the ground state using time-dependent DFT with B3LYP/6-31G*. GaussView (version 5) has been utilized as a visualization tool for the molecular orbitals and structures of GQDs [40]. The charge difference density, absorption and photoluminescence spectra have been plotted utilizing Multiwfn [41].

The spectacular electronic and optical properties of holey GQDs have been discussed in this section. First of all, the thermodynamic stability of the GQDs has been determined by calculating the cohesive energy of all the GQDs. The band gap calculation from HOMO and LUMO have all been discussed, followed by the determination of absorption, PL spectra, and Raman spectra.

The optimized structures in the ground state of all the considered holey GQDs (pores-created GQDs) have been represented in Fig. 1 , along with the cohesive energy in Table 1 . There are a total of 6 GQDs, 3 holey GQDs with their edges cut off with H atoms, and 3 holey GQDs with their edges cut off with F-atoms. These holey GQDs are obtained from GQD having 54 carbon (C) atoms. Fig. 1 (a) represents the GQD with a pore created by removing the inner 6-carbon atom, and the pore is further passivated with a hydrogen (H) atom. Similarly, in Fig. 1 (b) and (c), the pores of the GQD are passivated with an oxygen atom. Fig. 1 (d), (e), and (f) represent these pore-created GQDs with their edges passivated with fluorine (F) atoms. The F- atoms are out of the plane in alternate directions as a result of repulsive contact induced by their incredible electronegativity. This figure shows that the passivation of these holey GQDs somewhat distorts the structures, but all the structures are stable, as evidenced by the negative value of cohesive energy listed in Table 1.

The cohesive energy (
E C
) is defined as the energy required to break down the structure into its constituents, and therefore its negative values simply imply that the structure is thermodynamically stable. This can be calculated by using formula [42]:
E C
=
E
G Q D
−
N c
E c
−
N X
E X
−
N H
E H
N c
+
N X
+
N H

Where X stands for the O and F atoms,
E
G Q D
stands for the total energy of holey GQDs, and Ec, Ex, and EH stand for the atomic energy of C, X, and H, respectively. NC, NX, and NH have been used to denote the number of C, X, and H atoms.

The structural parameters, such as the bond length and bond angle of different atoms in all holey GQDs, have been calculated following the stability check. The C–C atom bond length has been observed to range from 1.36 Å to 1.45 Å for all GQDs, from 1.41 Å to 1.44 Å for C–O bonds, 1.08 Å for C–H bonds, and approximately 1.38 Å for C–F bonds which align with the previously reported results [20,21]. The bond angle of the C–C–C bond is different for all the GQDs and in the range from 113°-122o for hexagonal rings indicating that the hexagonal symmetry is slightly distorted when carbon atoms are replaced by any other atom. While for the pentagonal rings, it is 109o. The bond lengths and bond angles between various atoms have also been represented in Fig. 1. The bond length varies in the same way as studied by experiments, i.e. if a smaller atom is used in place of the C atom, the bond length will shorten [43,44], as indicated in this figure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electronic band gap and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para: The energy band gaps of all the holey GQDs are calculated as an energy difference between the HOMO and LUMO of these GQDs and are represented in Fig. 2 along with the isosurface contour plots of these orbitals. The band gap of pristine GQD with 54 carbon atoms is 2.85 eV [20], which increases when pores are created in this QD. Further, the band gap decreases when these pores are passivated by O-atoms instead of H-atoms, as represented in Fig. 2 (a) while Fig. 2 (b) represents all the GQDs passivated by F-atoms. When the edges of all these 3 holey GQDs are passivated with F-atoms, the energy band gap decreases, but this change is smaller in comparison to pore passivation. From this, one may conclude that energy band gap and absorption spectra have a greater dependence on pore passivation than edge passivation, which has also been discussed by other authors [45]. The larger decrement may be attributed to the fact that the O atom lowers the sp2 hybridization of C-atoms present in the GQDs as well as it is an electronegative element which lowers the energy band gap. While the F atom just only replace the H atoms and doesn't have any impact on hybridization, it onlyaffects the band gap because of its electronegativity.

The absorption spectra of these 3 holey GQDs have been presented in Fig. 3 (a) with their edges passivated with H-atoms and in Fig. 3 (b) with edges passivated with F-atoms. Fig. 3 (a) shows that the pores created in GQDs result in absorption peaks in the UV region. The absorption strength of holey GQDs diminishes when the H-atom is replaced by O-atoms because these atoms reduce the sp2 hybridized area of C-atoms, which contributes significantly to the absorption of light in GQDs. It may be verified by the isosurfaces of the orbitals involved in the maximum peak of the absorption spectra represented in Fig. 3 (c). All these surfaces show that the transitions taking place are π to π* transitions. From Fig. 3 (b), it is clear that edge passivation of these holey GQDs results in the red shift of all the peaks in absorption spectra, which is in consequence of our previous article on our passivation [20]. Also, the isosurface maps in Fig. 3 (d) show some σ distribution, but the major contribution is from π to π* transition, as evident in this figure.

Additionally, the numerical indices that measure electron excitation attributes have been generated to analyze the stimulation of electrons in various excited states using Multiwfn programmes. The distribution of the density of electron-hole pairs has also been displayed using the transition density matrix (TDM). The numerical values, including the Sr index, which measures the degree to which the electron and hole centres coincide, the D-index, which measures the separation between their centres; and the t-index, which assesses how much the hole and electron are separated from one another, have been computed and are displayed in Table 2 . A larger value of the Sr index implies a remarkable amount of overlap between the centres of the electrons and holes, whereas smaller values of D index and t index < 0 indicates a very small separation between them, and such values for a state demonstrate that the state is locally excites (LE) indicating that no charge transfer occured during the transition. Table 2 points out that the most probable excited states are LE.

The nature of excited states has also been confirmed by plotting transition density matrices (TDM). TDM contour maps additionally assure the accuracy of the data extracted from the quantitative analysis of all these indices. The most likely exciting states for each holey GQDs’ TDM have been shown in Fig. 4 . The numerical values on the x and y axes demonstrate the atom labels that make up the GQDs to represent the hole and electron positions, respectively. Examining the X-axis, which denotes the location of the hole, and the position of the electron is denoted by the Y-axis, would help us comprehend how electrons migrate between various atoms. As a result, the diagonal elements of the TDM contour map are characterized by the accumulation of the electron and hole at the same atom. The uniform disbursement of the electron-hole combination demonstrates all these states are LE.

Turning our attention to the photoluminescence (PL) spectra, the primary focus of this investigation because we can identify the emission wavelength of the examined GQDs through analysis of PL spectra. The highest peak of PL spectra corresponds to the emission wavelength of the GQD. This wavelength is crucial for determining the suitability of the GQD for specific applications. Fig. 5 (a) represents the PL spectra of the considered 3 holey GQDs. It is visible that the PL emission peaks of these GQDs are in the UVA (310 nm–400 nm) region with an additional peak in the visible region, which is not hazardous. This is a great achievement in this direction since neither GQDs are toxic nor they emit in the unsafe zone. Fig. 5 (b) simply represents that the F-atoms passivation results in the redshift of the peak, and these GQDs also have emissions in the UVA region. The isosurface maps shown in Fig. 5 (c) and (d) provide the same information as discussed in the case of absorption spectra.

In this article, a way to achieve non-hazardous UVA-emitting GQDs has been discussed. To achieve such GQDs, pores are created in between these GQDs, and their band gap and various optical properties have been analyzed. These pores significantly affect the band gaps and absorption and emission spectra of GQDs. It has been found that pore passivation has a noteworthy effect on modulating the band gap of GQDs as compared to edge passivation. From the absorption and photoluminescence spectra, it has been noted that the maximum contributing transitions are almost π to π* transitions. All the holey GQDs have emission peaks from 310 nm to 400 nm, with additional peaks in the visible region. In addition, these GQDs don't have any additional peaks in the high-energy UV region (<300 nm). Thus the obtained PL intensity is in the desired region, and hence these GQDs are very suitable for UV applications like medical therapy, and UV-A lamps in Forensic Investigations.

Priya Rani: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology. Sunita Srivastava: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the HPCC, Panjab University, Chandigarh, for facilitating the Gaussian 09 Package for the calculations. The authors also thank the Department of Physics, Guru Jambheshwar University of Science and Technology, Hisar, for providing the necessary computational facilities.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magical allotropes of carbon: prospects and applications
Crit. Rev. Solid State Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. K. Tiwari, V. Kumar, A. Huczko et al., “Magical allotropes of carbon: prospects and applications”, Crit. Rev. Solid State Mater. Sci., 41, 257, 2016.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inferring the energy sensitivity and band gap of electronic transport in a network of carbon nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Tang, “Inferring the energy sensitivity and band gap of electronic transport in a network of carbon nanotubes”, Sci. Rep., 12, 2060, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Systematic engineering of electronic and optical properties of graphene quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dots: an overview of synthesis, properties, and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Dalal, and S. Srivastava, “Study of Electronic and Optical Properties of Quantum Dots”, Appl. Nanoscience, 12, 2127, 2022.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Singh, and S. Srivastava, “Ab initio study of optical properties of ZnS quantum dots”, Mater. Today Proc., 46, 5817, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis, applications, and prospects of graphene quantum dots: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Ghaffarkhah, E. Hosseini, M. Kamkar et al., “Synthesis, Applications, and Prospects of Graphene Quantum Dots: A Comprehensive Review”, Nano-Micro Small, 18, 2102683, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots and its modified application for energy storage and conversion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Roy, A. Nagar, A. Sharma et al., “Graphene quantum dots and its modified application for energy storage and conversion”, J. Energy Storage, 39, 102606, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dot-integrated GaN light-emitting diodes with resolution beyond the retinal limit

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Bae, Y. Shin, H. Yoo et al., “Quantum dot-integrated GaN light-emitting diodes with resolution beyond the retinal limit”, Nature Communications, 13, 1862, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum size effect in three-dimensional microscopic semiconductor crystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. I. Ekimov and A. Onushchehko, “Quantum Size Effect in Three-Dimensional Microscopic Semiconductor Crystals” JETP letters, 34, 363, 1981.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum size effects in the redox potentials, resonance Raman spectra, and electronic spectra of CdS crystallites in aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Rossetti, S. Nakahara, and L. E. Brus, “Quantum size effects in the redox potentials, resonance Raman spectra, and electronic spectra of CdS crystallites in aqueous solution”, J. Chem. Phys., 79, 1086 (1983).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis and characterization of nearly monodisperse CdE (E = sulfur, selenium, tellurium) semiconductor nanocrystallites

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. B. Murray, D. J. Norris, and M. G. Bawendi, “Synthesis and characterization of nearly monodisperse CdE (E = sulfur, selenium, tellurium) semiconductor nanocrystallites”, J Am Chem. Soc., 115, 8706, 1993.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile and scalable synthesis of un-doped, doped and co-doped graphene quantum dots: a comparative study on their impact for environmental applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Suryawanshi, R. Kurrey, S. Sahu et al., “Facile and scalable synthesis of un-doped, doped and co-doped graphene quantum dots: a comparative study on their impact for environmental applications” RSC Adv., 13, 701, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-narrow-bandwidth graphene quantum dots for super resolved spectral and spatial sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, X. Dong, S. Zhou et al., “Ultra-narrow-bandwidth graphene quantum dots for super resolved spectral and spatial sensing”, NPG Asia Mater. 13, 5, 2021.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Zhang, Y. Tao, B. Tang et al., “Graphene Quantum Dots with Improved Fluorescence Activity via Machine Learning: Implications for Fluorescence Monitoring”, ACS Appl. Nano Mater, 5, 2728, 2022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots with red emission and high quantum yield

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. J. Jin, L. Tan, Z. Q. Zhao et al., “Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots with red emission and high quantum yield”, New J. Chem., 47, 2221, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning properties of graphene quantum dots by passivation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Dalal, S. Srivastava et al., “Tuning Properties of Graphene Quantum Dots by Passivation”, Phys. Chem. Chem. Phys., 24, 26232, 2022.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Dalal, and S. Srivastava, “Effect of surface modification on optical and electronic properties of Graphene Quantum Dots”, App. Surf. Sci., 609, 155379, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances on graphene quantum dots: from chemistry and Physics to applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Yan, J. Gong, J. Chen et al., “Recent Advances on Graphene Quantum Dots: From Chemistry and Physics to Applications”, Adv. mater., 31, 1808283, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced NIR fluorescence quantum yield of graphene quantum dots using dopants

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rani, R. Dalal, and S. Srivastava, “Enhanced NIR fluorescence quantum yield of graphene quantum dots using dopants”, Physica Scripta, 98, 064004, 2023.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: How does the porphyrin-like vacancy affect the spectral properties of graphene quantum dots: a theoretical study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. C. Li, W. Jian, Y. Li et al., “How does the porphyrin-like vacancy affect the spectral properties of graphene quantum dots: a theoretical study”, J. Phys. Condens. Matter, 32, 155902, 2020.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. S. Eldeeb, M. M. Fadlallah, G. J. Martyna et al., “Doping of large-pore crown graphene nanomesh”, Carbon, 133, 369, 2018.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Mombru, M. Romero, R. Faccio et al., “Curvature and vacancies in graphene quantum dots”, Appl. Surf. Sci. 462, 540, 2018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene quantum dots induced porous orientation of holey graphene nanosheets for improved electrocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Ali, R. Riaz, A. S. Anjum et al., “Graphene quantum dots induced porous orientation of holey graphene nanosheets for improved electrocatalytic activity”, Carbon, 171, 493, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Vanadium nitride quantum dots/holey graphene matrix boosting adsorption and conversion reaction kinetics for high-performance Lithium−Sulfur batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Li, M. Zhang, W. Chen et al., “Vanadium Nitride Quantum Dots/Holey Graphene Matrix Boosting Adsorption and Conversion Reaction Kinetics for High-Performance Lithium−Sulfur Batteries”, ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces, 13, 30746, 2021.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Kasry, M. M. Fadlallah, N. H. Voelcker et al., “Experimental and theoretical demonstrations of ultraviolet absorption enhancement in porous nano-membrane graphene”, Carbon 155, 65, 2019.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Application of ultraviolet, visible, and infrared light imaging in protein-based biopharmaceutical formulation characterization and development studies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. E. Klijn and J. Hubbuch, “Application of ultraviolet, visible, and infrared light imaging in protein-based biopharmaceutical formulation characterization and development studies”, Euro. J. Pharmaceutics and Biopharmaceutics, 165, 319, 2021.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-Power genuine ultraviolet light-emitting diodes based on colloidal nanocrystal quantum dots
